retrieved_docs.content: Catch-up vaccination for Haemophilus influenza is not recommended for previously unvaccinated children above 5 years of age without risk factors.

retrieved_docs.title: Understanding and Application of CDC Immunization Guidelines -- Issues of Concern -- 2020 CDC Immunization Schedule Updates
retrieved_docs.content: If a DTaP or Tdap vaccine is given at 10 years of age for other reasons, it can be included as the booster dose in the CDC schedule. If the DTaP or Tdap vaccine is given at 7 to 9 years of age, we should not include it as the booster dose, and Tdap should be readministered at eleven to twelve years of age.

retrieved_docs.title: Understanding and Application of CDC Immunization Guidelines -- Clinical Significance -- Pneumococcal Vaccination
retrieved_docs.content: For age groups 7 to 23 months, a 2-dose series is recommended with a 12 weeks interval and a second dose given after 12 months. For unimmunized healthy children aged 2 to 5 years, 1 dose is recommended.

retrieved_docs.content: The 5th dose of the DTaP vaccine is unnecessary if the fourth dose was given after 4 years of age and 6 months after the third dose.

retrieved_docs.title: Understanding and Application of CDC Immunization Guidelines -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Two doses are recommended at 12 to 15 months and 4 to 6 years. Twelve months is the minimum age for the first dose, and a 4-week minimum interval is needed between the first and second doses. MMR vaccine can be given to children 6 to 11 months old who need to travel to countries with high endemicity for measles. However, this dose does not count toward the 2-dose series. For unvaccinated individuals, 2 doses 4 weeks apart are recommended. MMR vaccine can be used as postexposure prophylaxis within 3 days of exposure.

retrieved_docs.title: Understanding and Application of CDC Immunization Guidelines -- Issues of Concern -- 2020 CDC Immunization Schedule Updates
retrieved_docs.content: The age group 19 to 21 years and 22 to 26 years are combined as 19 to 26 years in the 2020 CDC schedule due to the change in the recommendation of catch-up HPV vaccination for all individuals aged up to 26.

retrieved_docs.title: Understanding and Application of CDC Immunization Guidelines -- Issues of Concern -- Annual Updates in the CDC Immunization Schedule
retrieved_docs.content: CDC issues annual updates in immunization schedules. [7] New vaccines were added in some of the annual updates, eg, Chickenpox (1996), hepatitis A (2000), pneumococcal vaccine (2001). Few vaccines were discontinued from the schedule, eg, the oral polio vaccine (2004). New versions of existing vaccines were added, eg, DTaP (1997), intranasal influenza (2004). Additional recommendations were advised for already existing vaccines. The current immunization schedules for children below 6 years comprises vaccines that prevent 14 infectious diseases, which is a drastic achievement compared with the schedule in 1948 when immunizations against only diphtheria, tetanus, pertussis, and smallpox were available. Per the immunization schedule, a child may receive up to 24 injections in the first 2 years of age and up to 5 injections in a single visit.

retrieved_docs.title: Understanding and Application of CDC Immunization Guidelines -- Clinical Significance -- Influenza Vaccination
retrieved_docs.content: The minimum age for the influenza vaccine is 6 months for the inactivated vaccine and 2 years for the live vaccine. For the age group 6 months to 8 years, 2 doses 4 weeks apart are recommended for those getting them for the first time. In subsequent years, kids up to 8 years can get only 1 dose annually. One dose is recommended for all individuals above 9 years of age. Individuals with egg allergies can get the influenza vaccine under medical supervision. retrieved_docs.content: CDC 2000 schedule recommends the HPV vaccine for individuals 11 to 26 years of age, irrespective of gender. Catch-up HPV vaccination is recommended for those not fully vaccinated up to 26. For individuals between 27 and 45 years, shared decision-making for the HPV vaccine is recommended.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 76
retrieved_docs.content: The DTPP vaccine is classified as a member of the combined bacterial and viral vaccines group, meaning it incorporates antigens that protect against both bacterial infections (such as diphtheria, tetanus, and pertussis) and viral infections (such as poliomyelitis). By belonging to this category of combination vaccines, the DTPP vaccine enables simultaneous immunization against multiple pathogens, streamlining vaccination schedules and improving patient adherence to preventive care.

This categorization highlights the vaccine’s role in delivering protection against several diseases—such as diphtheria, tetanus, pertussis, and polio—through one combined formulation, which can simplify vaccination schedules and improve patient compliance.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2235
retrieved_docs.content: Injection of RhD immune globulin is classified as a form of التمنيع السلبي (passive immunization). Within the broader framework of human vaccination, التمنيع السلبي represents a child category, indicating that passive immunization is considered a subset of vaccination strategies applied to humans. Consequently, the administration of RhD immune globulin exemplifies how passive immunization techniques are incorporated into human vaccination programs to provide immediate, short‑term protection, particularly in contexts such as preventing hemolytic disease of the newborn. This relationship underscores the role of passive immunization as an integral component of comprehensive vaccination approaches in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2518
retrieved_docs.content: Immunizations and screening for infectious disease are linked to فحص التوبركولين إيجابي, indicating that tuberculin testing is a component of infectious‑disease preventive care. In addition, حج القحف is clinically associated with فحص التوبركولين إيجابي, suggesting that measurements of head circumference may be relevant when evaluating a positive tuberculin test, particularly in contexts such as pediatric tuberculosis where cranial growth patterns can reflect disease involvement. Together, these relationships highlight the role of tuberculin screening within broader immunization programs and the potential clinical significance of head‑circumference assessment in patients with a positive tuberculin result.
